verses.text: Sarah lived 127 years — the only woman in Scripture whose age at death is recorded. She dies at Hebron, and Abraham comes to mourn and weep for her. The promise wife, the laughing mother, is gone.
Sarah lived 127 years — the only woman in Scripture whose age at death is recorded. She dies at Hebron, and Abraham comes to mourn and weep for her. The promise wife, the laughing mother, is gone.

Abraham rises from beside her body and approaches the Hittites. I am a stranger and sojourner with you, he says — give me a burying place. After a lifetime in this land, he still has no soil he can call his own.

Abraham rises from beside her body and approaches the Hittites. I am a stranger and sojourner with you, he says — give me a burying place. After a lifetime in this land, he still has no soil he can call his own.

They answer with deep respect: you are a prince of God among us; bury her in the choicest of our tombs; none of us will refuse. Abraham's standing in the community is high. But what he wants is something they can't simply give.

They answer with deep respect: you are a prince of God among us; bury her in the choicest of our tombs; none of us will refuse. Abraham's standing in the community is high. But what he wants is something they can't simply give.

Abraham bows and asks them to plead his case with Ephron son of Zohar — for the cave of Machpelah at the edge of his field. I will pay full price, he says. He doesn't want a gift; he wants a legal title.

Abraham bows and asks them to plead his case with Ephron son of Zohar — for the cave of Machpelah at the edge of his field. I will pay full price, he says. He doesn't want a gift; he wants a legal title.

Abraham bows and asks them to plead his case with Ephron son of Zohar — for the cave of Machpelah at the edge of his field. I will pay full price, he says. He doesn't want a gift; he wants a legal title.

Ephron is sitting right there at the gate. He answers publicly: I give you the field, and the cave in it. Take it — bury your dead. The Eastern bargaining ritual has begun. Generous words; the price will come.

Ephron is sitting right there at the gate. He answers publicly: I give you the field, and the cave in it. Take it — bury your dead. The Eastern bargaining ritual has begun. Generous words; the price will come.
Abraham bows again. He insists: let me give you the money. I will pay for the field. Take it from me, and then I will bury my dead. He will not accept the land as charity.
Abraham bows again. He insists: let me give you the money. I will pay for the field. Take it from me, and then I will bury my dead. He will not accept the land as charity.
Ephron names a price wrapped in politeness: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver — but what is that between friends? Bury your dead. The friendly tone hides a steep figure.
Ephron names a price wrapped in politeness: the land is worth four hundred shekels of silver — but what is that between friends? Bury your dead. The friendly tone hides a steep figure.
Abraham weighs out four hundred shekels of silver, current with the merchant. He pays the full price without bargaining. The land must be undisputedly his.
The field of Ephron in Machpelah, with its cave and every tree in it, was made over to Abraham in the presence of all the Hittites at the gate. The transfer is public, witnessed, and final.
The field of Ephron in Machpelah, with its cave and every tree in it, was made over to Abraham in the presence of all the Hittites at the gate. The transfer is public, witnessed, and final.

Abraham buries Sarah in the cave. The field and the cave are now his by purchased right. This is Abraham's first owned piece of the promised land — a burial plot. The down payment on the promise is also a grave.
